name: Optimizing mat_mul runtime performance for LINAL linear algebra library

description: Baseline: default -O2 -march=native, standard i-k-j loop ordering with memset + compute pass. Total 18,789 µs across sizes 4-256 (square + rect). Correctness verified against naive reference.

hypothesis.baseline: standard matmul with i-k-j loop ordering, separate memset for zero-init

bottleneck_analysis.small_matrices: too fast to measure at ms resolution - overhead dominated
bottleneck_analysis.cache_behavior: i-k-j ordering gives good B access pattern; A is streamed once per k

description: Discard: TILE=8 tiling made mat_mul 44% slower across all sizes. Extra loop nesting overhead dominates for matrices ≤256×256. Correctness preserved.

analysis.tile_8_too_small: Overhead of extra loop nesting for TILE=8 outweighed cache benefits. Matrices up to 256x256 (512KB doubles) likely fit in L2/L3 cache already, so blocking helped little.
next_actions: Try larger TILE=16 or TILE=32 to reduce loop overhead
Consider that auto-vectorization at -O2 may already be optimal for these sizes

description: Discard: TILE=16 tiling still 24% slower than baseline. Loop overhead dominates cache benefits at -O2 for matrices ≤256×256. Tile size increase from 8→16 helped but not enough.

analysis.tile_trend: 8→16 improved from +44% to +24%, suggesting larger tiles could work but need TILE≥32+ which adds complexity
analysis.root_cause: -O2 auto-vectorization of simple loops is already good; tiling overhead outweighs cache benefits for these matrix sizes
next_actions: Try pointer arithmetic instead of index calculations in inner loop
Try manual unrolling of j-loop (4x or 8x)
Consider that -O2 may be the bottleneck — but we can't change build flags

description: Discard: pointer arithmetic + 4x unrolling gave marginal -1.4% (worse). Small matrices improved (64×64: -25%) but large ones regressed (256×256: +13%). Compiler auto-vectorizer at -O2 handles simple loops better for large matrices.

analysis.small_matrices_benefit: Pointer arithmetic reduces address calc overhead for small matrices
analysis.large_matrices_hurt: Manual unrolling at 4x prevents compiler from generating wider SIMD (AVX) instructions that process 4 doubles at once vs our 2 doubles per iteration pair
next_actions: Try with __restrict + __builtin_assume_aligned hints for better auto-vectorization
Try unroll factor 8 or let compiler decide with pragmas
Consider that simple loop may already be near-optimal at -O2

description: Discard: restrict + __builtin_assume_aligned(ptr, 32) made mat_mul 8% slower. Data from malloc() isn't 32-byte aligned; compiler generates aligned AVX instructions that perform poorly on misaligned data.

analysis.assume_aligned_hurts: malloc data is typically 16-byte aligned on x86_64, not 32. The compiler generated aligned AVX instructions that perform poorly on misaligned accesses. This is a critical finding: do NOT assume 32-byte alignment for malloc'd memory.
next_actions: Try just restrict without alignment hints
Consider that simple loop at -O2 may already be near-optimal

description: Discard: restrict pointers only gave marginal -4% (worse). Pointer arithmetic overhead slightly exceeds any alias-analysis benefit at -O2. Simple index-based loops are already well-optimized by GCC's auto-vectorizer.

hypothesis.restrict_only: local restrict pointers with same loop structure

analysis.key_finding: GCC -O2 auto-vectorizer already handles simple index loops very well. Pointer arithmetic adds overhead that exceeds any benefit from restrict alias hints.
patterns_observed: Tiling always hurts (loop overhead > cache benefits at these sizes)
Pointer+unroll helps small but hurts large
next_directions: Try i-j-k loop ordering (register accumulation, fewer stores)
Consider that baseline may already be near-optimal at -O2

description: Discard: i-j-k loop ordering is 2.9× SLOWER than baseline. Column-wise B access causes massive cache misses at all sizes (256×256: 36.8ms vs 10.3ms). Confirms i-k-j is the correct ordering for row-major matrices.

hypothesis.ij_k_ordering: i-j-k loop with register accumulation per output element

analysis.root_cause: Column-wise access on B destroys cache locality. For row-major matrices, i-k-j is provably optimal among the three orderings.
analysis.conclusion: i-k-j is the best loop ordering for row-major matrices at -O2. Stop trying different orderings.

description: Discard: k-i-j ordering 10% slower than baseline. Good B reuse but poor C temporal locality — streams all C rows per B row, evicting cache lines. i-k-j remains best loop ordering for row-major matrices at -O2.

hypothesis.ki_j_ordering: k-i-j: group by B rows, process all C rows per B row

orderings_tested.i_k_j_baseline: best — sequential C row access, good temporal locality
orderings_tested.k_i_j: worse — streams all C rows per B row, poor C reuse
orderings_tested.i_j_k: much worse — column-wise B access destroys cache

analysis.conclusion: Loop ordering is not the bottleneck. The i-k-j structure is optimal. Need to look at other factors: compiler optimization level, vectorization width, memory bandwidth.
next_directions: Consider SIMD intrinsics for wider vectorization (AVX = 4 doubles vs SSE 2)
